homelandThreats.title: Austin Bar Shooting — Possible Iran-Motivated Attack
homelandThreats.text: On March 1, Ndiaga Diagne (53, naturalized US citizen from Senegal) opened fire on Buford's Backyard Beer Garden on W. 6th Street in Austin, TX, killing 3 and wounding 15 before being fatally shot by police. He wore a shirt with an Iranian flag design and a "Property of Allah" hoodie. Search warrant at his home revealed an Iranian flag and photos of Iranian regime leaders. FBI investigating as possible terrorism linked to US-Iran conflict. Authorities caution motive is not yet confirmed.
homelandThreats.sources: CBS News, CNN, Washington Post, TIME, Fox News

homelandThreats.title: FBI on High Alert — Nationwide Sleeper Cell Monitoring
homelandThreats.text: FBI Director Kash Patel mobilized counterterrorism and intelligence teams nationwide following the start of Operation Epic Fury. Joint Terrorism Task Forces are working to monitor and disrupt potential Iran-linked plots. Federal and local law enforcement have boosted security in major US cities. DHS issued an internal law enforcement bulletin warning of potential lone-wolf and cyberattacks — but has NOT issued a public NTAS (National Terrorism Advisory System) alert, drawing criticism from security experts.
homelandThreats.sources: Fox News, Fortune, HSToday, NPR, Newsmax

homelandThreats.title: Iran/Hezbollah Sleeper Cell Network — Documented Locations
homelandThreats.location: Houston TX, Los Angeles CA, Boston MA, Portland OR, Louisville KY
homelandThreats.text: US intelligence has documented Iranian and Hezbollah-linked sleeper cells across the United States over the past decade. These cells have been observed in Houston (explosive precursor stockpiling), Los Angeles (surveillance of Israeli/Jewish institutions), Boston (fundraising and logistics), Portland (recruitment), and Louisville (logistics). With Operation Epic Fury underway, all cells are under heightened surveillance by FBI Joint Terrorism Task Forces.
homelandThreats.sources: HSToday, JNS, Jerusalem Post, Fox News

homelandThreats.title: Iran Cyberattack Threat — 60+ Hacktivist Groups Active
homelandThreats.text: More than 60 hacktivist groups linked to Iran have been activated since the start of the conflict. Targets include US government systems, power utilities, financial networks, and healthcare infrastructure. Iran's own internet is down to 1-4% capacity following US strikes on communications infrastructure. US CISA is reportedly stretched thin. Palo Alto Unit 42 warns of likely escalation in cyberattacks against civilian targets.
homelandThreats.sources: Palo Alto Unit 42, Fortune, CNBC, CSIS, Nextgov

homelandThreats.title: State & Local Security Posture Elevated Across US
homelandThreats.text: State and local law enforcement agencies have elevated their security posture in response to the Iran conflict. Increased police presence at critical infrastructure, government buildings, and gathering places reported in New York, California, Texas, Florida, and other major states. Some states have activated National Guard support for key installations. State fusion centers are coordinating with the FBI and DHS to share intelligence on potential threats.

homelandThreats.title: No DHS NTAS Alert Issued — Security Experts Critical
homelandThreats.text: Despite the largest US military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion, the Department of Homeland Security has NOT issued a public National Terrorism Advisory System (NTAS) alert. The last NTAS bulletin was issued on June 22, 2025. Security experts have criticized this decision, noting that internal law enforcement bulletins have been issued but the public remains uninformed about the elevated threat landscape.

evidence.title: Enrichment Levels
evidence.text: Iran enriched uranium to 60% purity at Fordow underground facility. Rate: 1.1 kg/day as of May 2025. From 60% to 90% (weapons-grade) is a straightforward technical step — estimated weeks to months.

evidence.title: Stockpile Size
evidence.text: 408.6 kg of 60% enriched UF6 as of May 2025. Additionally: 274.5 kg at 20% enrichment, 5,508.8 kg at 5% enrichment. Far exceeds JCPOA limits (capped at 300 kg of 3.67%-enriched material).

evidence.title: Unexplained Uranium Particles
evidence.text: IAEA found traces of enriched uranium at undeclared sites in Iran. Iran has failed to provide a credible explanation. Could indicate undisclosed military nuclear research or weapons development activity.

evidence.title: Fordow Underground Facility
evidence.text: Built inside a mountain near Qom, originally covert until exposed in 2009. Designed to withstand aerial bombardment — its very construction indicates Iran anticipated potential strikes on its nuclear program.

evidence.title: Loss of IAEA Access
evidence.text: Since June 2025 Israeli strikes (Operation Midnight Hammer), the IAEA has been unable to conduct in-field verification. Current enrichment status, stockpile location, and facility damage are all unknown.

evidence.title: Breakout Timeline
evidence.text: Pre-strike estimate: weeks to a few months to produce enough weapons-grade uranium for one device. Depends on operational centrifuge capacity. Current capability unknown due to strike damage to Natanz and Fordow.

regionalReactions.text: 73% of Israeli Jews support strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities. Widespread concern about Hezbollah retaliation from the north. Rally-around-flag effect among the public, but growing anxiety over regional escalation.

Gulf states had privately lobbied for US action against Iran's nuclear program pre-strike. Now expressing concern about escalation and blowback. Worried about oil market instability, Houthi attacks, and Red Sea shipping disruptions.

Mixed sentiment among Iranian diaspora communities. Some support regime change through military intervention. Others oppose foreign military action against Iran on principle, regardless of views on the Islamic Republic.

Divided response: France urges caution, Germany calls for restraint, UK broadly supports US action, Italy and Spain remain neutral. EU publicly aligned with US but privately concerned about escalation and refugee flows.

Significant anti-war protests in NYC, Washington DC, Los Angeles, Chicago. Large demonstrations in London, Berlin, Paris, Madrid. Rising global anti-war sentiment, particularly among younger demographics.
Both voted against ceasefire at UN Emergency Session. Russia condemns strikes as 'unprovoked aggression.' China calls for 'immediate restraint.' Neither has threatened direct military intervention.

Al Jazeera’s updated live tracker says CENTCOM reported more than 5,000 US strikes on targets in Iran by March 10, and AP reported on March 13 that the US is reinforcing the theater with 2,500 Marines and the USS Tripoli after nearly two weeks of war. briefings.summary: Confirmed reporting available in searched sources shows Iran’s official death toll at 1,444 and wounded at 18,551 as of March 13, 2026, while at least 15 Israelis, 11 US service members, and 19 people in Gulf states have been reported killed. Al Jazeera’s updated live tracker says CENTCOM reported more than 5,000 US strikes on targets in Iran by March 10, and AP reported on March 13 that the US is reinforcing the theater with 2,500 Marines and the USS Tripoli after nearly two weeks of war. Several requested military-usage totals, including Tomahawks, JDAMs, JASSMs, SDBs, B-2 sorties, MOPs, fighter sorties, and interceptor firings, could not be confirmed from named current sources in the searched results, so they remain null.

As of March 12, 2026, the clearest confirmed cumulative figures located are 1,348 people killed and 17,000 wounded in Iran from reporting carried by Al Jazeera’s live tracker citing Iran’s UN representative, 15 Israelis killed in Iranian attacks, and 7 U.S. service members confirmed killed in combat according to a March 11 CENTCOM update reported by Stars and Stripes; the same CENTCOM update said U.S. forces had struck more than 5,500 targets in Iran. Official material confirms carriers, destroyers, MQ-9s, Patriot and THAAD systems are in use, but accessible primary-source reporting reviewed did not provide reliable current totals for deployed force counts or cumulative weapons expenditures such as Tomahawks, JDAMs, JASSMs, SDBs, MOPs, sorties, or interceptor launches.

Confirmed reporting on March 11, 2026 shows the cumulative Iranian death toll at 1,255 and wounded at 12,000 according to Iran’s deputy health minister, while U.S. forces have struck over 5,500 targets in Iran according to CENTCOM commander Adm. Brad Cooper. Pentagon/CENTCOM-linked reporting says eight U.S. service members have died since the start of Operation Epic Fury, and Israeli reporting cited by Al Jazeera puts Israeli deaths at 13. Confirmed Gulf-state deaths total 17 across Bahrain, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, and Oman. New confirmed developments in today’s reporting include attacks on oil shipping and port infrastructure in Iraqi and Omani waters, continuing concern over civilian harm including the Minab school strike, and a claimed Iran-linked retaliatory cyberattack on Stryker.

Confirmed reporting on March 10, 2026 indicates the Pentagon has acknowledged about 140 US service members wounded and 7 US military fatalities from Iranian attacks, while CENTCOM's latest publicly located fact sheet says US forces had struck over 3,000 targets in the first 7 days of Operation Epic Fury. Publicly accessible reporting located in this search also states that strikes on Iran had killed more than 1,250 other people in addition to Ali Khamenei, supporting an Iranian death total of at least 1,251. I could not confirm newer official totals for Israeli deaths, Iranian civilian-only deaths, Iranian wounded, Gulf-state deaths, or the requested weapons-deployment/use counts from named primary sources.

As of Monday, March 9, 2026, the clearest confirmed casualty count located in current reporting is from Iran’s deputy health minister Ali Jafarian, who told Al Jazeera that 1,255 people had been killed and more than 12,000 wounded in Iran after nine days of US-Israeli attacks, with officials saying most victims were civilians. Al Jazeera’s latest cross-border live tracker lists at least 13 people killed in Israel, 14 killed in Gulf states, and eight US soldiers killed, but the latest official CENTCOM statement found only confirms seven US service members killed in action as of March 8. For strike totals, the most specific official figure found was CENTCOM’s statement, cited by Al Jazeera, that US forces had struck more than 3,000 targets in Iran by March 7; no current official combined US-Israeli cumulative strike number was found in the searched sources.

dailyBriefing.title: Day 13 Intelligence Briefing — March 13, 2026
dailyBriefing.content:
The most consequential development in the past 24 hours is the apparent transition from a shock-and-awe opening phase into a sustained coercive campaign: U.S. officials now say more than 6,000 targets have been struck since Operation Epic Fury began on February 28, while Iran’s new supreme leader has publicly signaled no near-term de-escalation and continued pressure on Gulf shipping. Markets are reacting accordingly, with crude holding around the $100-per-barrel threshold and shipping disruption through the Strait of Hormuz still acting as the conflict’s main global economic transmission channel. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/99a2316e9089b2ffa9eadd104e787591?utm_source=openai))

## Military Operations Summary

In the last 24 hours, no single independently verified battlefield event appears to match the scale of the war’s opening days, but the operational picture remains one of high-tempo, multi-domain attrition. The most important fresh U.S. data point is cumulative: American forces have now struck more than 6,000 targets in Iran since February 28, according to U.S. military reporting cited by AP, including more than 30 minelaying vessels. Earlier Pentagon/CENTCOM fact sheets indicate the campaign has employed a broad mix of B-1, B-2 and B-52 bombers; F-15, F-16, F-18, F-22 and F-35 fighters; EA-18G electronic attack aircraft; MQ-9s; P-8 maritime patrol aircraft; RC-135 reconnaissance aircraft; HIMARS; carrier strike assets; guided-missile destroyers; Patriot and THAAD batteries; and counter-drone systems. Target categories identified by U.S. materials include command-and-control nodes, IRGC headquarters, ballistic missile sites, integrated air defenses, anti-ship missile sites, submarines, naval vessels and military communications infrastructure. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/99a2316e9089b2ffa9eadd104e787591?utm_source=openai))

On the Iranian side, Tehran continues to frame its response under “Operation True Promise 4,” with state-aligned outlets claiming additional missile and drone pressure against U.S. bases and Israeli targets and continued efforts to threaten or control shipping in the Gulf. However, in the past 24 hours, there are no independently confirmed reports of a major new Iranian strike wave comparable to earlier salvos. What is confirmed is that traffic through the Strait of Hormuz remains near-halted and that commercial shipping remains under threat, suggesting Iran continues to derive strategic effect from maritime disruption even absent a newly documented mass attack. This naval dimension remains central: by U.S. account, coalition operations have hit Iranian minelaying assets, while multiple recent reports indicate the strait remains constrained by attack risk and possible mine deployment. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/03/12/oil-prices-iran-strait-of-hormuz?utm_source=openai))

## What Each Side Is Reporting

Washington’s public line remains that Operation Epic Fury is systematically dismantling Iranian military capacity. The White House has described the campaign as intended to eliminate the nuclear threat, degrade ballistic missile forces, cripple naval assets and dismantle the regime’s security apparatus. CENTCOM-linked materials emphasize thousands of targets struck and broad suppression of air defense, missile and naval systems. The U.S. narrative is one of sustained operational success, technological overmatch and expanding control over the battlespace. ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/articles/2026/03/peace-through-strength-president-trump-launches-operation-epic-fury-to-crush-iranian-regime-end-nuclear-threat/?utm_source=openai))

Iranian state and state-aligned media present almost the mirror opposite picture. Press TV has claimed Iran degraded U.S. missile defense architecture, damaged THAAD-related radar and communications sites, imposed significant costs on U.S. regional basing, and successfully struck American and Israeli targets with systems including Khorramshahr, Fattah and Kheibar missiles alongside one-way attack drones. It also portrays U.S.-Israeli strikes as heavily focused on civilian infrastructure, including schools, hospitals and sports facilities. These claims should be treated cautiously: many are not independently verified, and Iranian media have a strong incentive to project resilience, deterrent credibility and rising U.S. costs. ([presstv.ir](https://www.presstv.ir/Detail/2026/03/07/765076/blinded-giant-how-iran-crippled-mutli-billion-dollar-thaad-network-in-days?utm_source=openai))

Israel’s reporting remains closely aligned with Washington’s broader strategic line: Iran and its proxy network are being targeted across theaters, including Lebanon, and Hezbollah’s entry into the war is used to justify continued northern-front operations. The core contradiction remains stark. U.S./Israeli reporting stresses precision and military degradation; Iranian reporting stresses civilian harm, successful retaliation and U.S. vulnerability. The likely analytic judgment is that both sides are selectively truthful: U.S./coalition forces probably have achieved major cumulative destruction inside Iran, while Tehran has also succeeded in imposing real costs through missiles, drones and maritime disruption, though likely less decisively than its media claims. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/articles-operation-roaring-lion/operating-on-the-northern-front-during-operation-roaring-lion-why-the-idf-is-operating-in-lebanon-today/?utm_source=openai))

## Market & Economic Impact

Oil remains the clearest real-time barometer of war risk. In the past 24 hours, crude has hovered around the $100 mark, with AP reporting prices at or above that threshold after Iran’s leadership vowed to continue fighting and keep pressure on Gulf shipping. Earlier this week Brent briefly surged near $120 per barrel, its highest since 2022, before pulling back; Reuters reporting earlier in the week, via secondary pickup, cited the U.S. Energy Information Administration forecasting Brent would remain above $95 for the next two months because of war-related supply disruption. The market message is that traders are now pricing not merely battlefield escalation, but prolonged transport disruption and a nontrivial Hormuz outage risk. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/74bf078b7e40b6a0d38033c9fe29a87b?utm_source=openai))

Equities remain risk-off, especially in Asia and among emerging markets. AP reported Asian shares were mostly lower on Friday, tracking prior Wall Street weakness amid concern over inflation, energy costs and supply disruption. Reuters reporting from earlier in the conflict showed emerging-market equity funds among the worst performers as investors cut exposure to risk assets. Safe-haven and inflation-sensitive trades remain in focus, with higher fuel costs feeding concerns over transport, manufacturing and food-price pass-through. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/74bf078b7e40b6a0d38033c9fe29a87b?utm_source=openai))

Government intervention risk is also rising. Axios reported the Trump administration is preparing to tap the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, though the reserve stood at about 415 million barrels as of March 6, relatively low by historical standards. That suggests policymakers are trying to cap gasoline and inflation fallout, but the reserve is not large enough to offset a long-duration Hormuz disruption if Gulf exports remain constrained. The main economic takeaway for Day 13 is that markets are no longer trading this as a short, containable strike campaign; they are trading a war premium with shipping risk at the center. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/03/11/trump-strategic-oil-reserve-gas-prices-iran-war?utm_source=openai))

## Consumer & Supply Chain Effects

Consumer-level stress is rising, though hard evidence of broad panic buying in Asia-Pacific over the last 24 hours remains limited. No confirmed reports in the last 24 hours establish widespread, region-wide fuel hoarding across Asia on a scale comparable to wartime panic episodes elsewhere. What is confirmed is the underlying driver: shipping through the Strait of Hormuz remains at a near halt, and tanker availability and freight costs have been sharply disrupted since the conflict escalated. Reuters reporting earlier in the crisis said Middle East supertanker costs hit all-time highs, while other shipping data showed vessel traffic through Hormuz collapsing and cargo volumes dropping steeply. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/2026/03/12/oil-prices-iran-strait-of-hormuz?utm_source=openai))

For Asia-Pacific importers, the vulnerability is direct. Much of East and South Asia depends on Gulf crude and LNG, so even absent visible gasoline lines, importers are facing higher spot prices, delayed cargo scheduling, insurance complications and rerouting costs. AP noted Friday that rising energy prices are already hurting consumers globally, while analysts are warning that fuel cost inflation will bleed into electronics, industrial production and shipping-intensive sectors. That is particularly relevant for Northeast Asian refiners and Southeast Asian logistics hubs, which are highly exposed to Middle Eastern energy flows. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/74bf078b7e40b6a0d38033c9fe29a87b?utm_source=openai))

The supply-chain effect is therefore more clearly documented than retail hoarding. Commercial shipping companies have slowed or suspended transits, and policymakers are considering naval escort arrangements to reopen lanes. If the current pattern persists into next week, the likely next-order effects are visible fuel rationing or precautionary stockpiling in import-dependent Asian markets, but as of March 13 there is insufficient confirmed reporting to state that this has become widespread. ([investing.com](https://www.investing.com/news/commodities-news/global-oil-and-gas-shipping-costs-surge-as-iran-vows-to-close-strait-of-hormuz-4537062?utm_source=openai))

## Diplomatic Developments

Diplomatically, the past 24 hours show motion but not breakthrough. The most concrete recent development is at the United Nations: the Security Council has demanded that Iran halt attacks on Gulf states, passing a resolution by 13 votes with China and Russia abstaining. That vote underscores a widening international consensus against Tehran’s attacks on neighboring states and shipping, even as neither Beijing nor Moscow has aligned with the U.S.-Israeli military campaign. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/24243b5c1e1f32ea8ff52b9a02822396?utm_source=openai))

On ceasefire prospects, the outlook remains poor. Iran’s foreign minister said earlier in the conflict that Tehran was not seeking a ceasefire or negotiations with Washington, and the new supreme leader’s first public message this week reinforced a hard-line position centered on continued resistance and leverage via the Strait of Hormuz and attacks on U.S. regional basing. No confirmed reports in the last 24 hours indicate active, near-term ceasefire talks producing substantive movement. ([tbsnews.net](https://www.tbsnews.net/world/tehran-says-no-ceasefire-refuses-negotiations-us-1378746?utm_source=openai))

China and Russia continue to occupy a familiar middle ground: condemning the spread of hostilities and calling for military operations to stop, but stopping short of direct intervention on Iran’s behalf. Reuters pickup from March 3 quoted China’s foreign ministry urging all parties to stop military operations in Iran, while broader reporting indicates both Beijing and Moscow are offering diplomatic cover rather than material escalation. Gulf Arab states, meanwhile, have moved decisively against Iran diplomatically after absorbing direct strikes, increasing the pressure on Tehran and reducing its regional room for maneuver. ([reutersconnect.com](https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/chinas-foreign-ministry-urges-all-parties-to-stop-military-operations-in-iran/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX1ZBMDMzMjAzMDMyMDI2UlAx?utm_source=openai))

## Humanitarian Situation

The humanitarian picture continues to deteriorate, though the most reliable publicly available figures still lag real-time events. UNHCR said on March 5 that more than 330,000 people had already been displaced by recent hostilities in the broader region, with no major cross-border outflow from Iran yet confirmed at that stage. Subsequent reporting citing UNHCR estimates said displacement across Iran and Lebanon had risen to roughly 767,700 by March 11. The UN also said earlier that estimates of people fleeing Tehran in the first two days had already surpassed 100,000. ([unhcr.org](https://www.unhcr.org/news/press-releases/unhcr-over-330-000-displaced-recent-hostilities-middle-east-and-beyond?utm_source=openai))

On civilian harm, verified information remains fragmentary and politically contested. The UN on March 3 reported sustained airstrikes had affected more than 1,000 locations in Iran, with about 790 deaths and nearly 750 injured at that point, though that early figure likely no longer captures the full scale of casualties. WHO said it had verified 13 attacks on health-care sites in Iran as of March 5. Iranian officials and aligned media claim much higher civilian and infrastructure damage, including strikes on schools and hospitals; those claims are plausible in part but not all independently verified. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/sg/en/content/highlight/2026-03-03.html?utm_source=openai))

The most defensible analytic assessment is that civilian suffering is substantial and rising, particularly in urban areas affected by strikes, displacement and service disruption. ICRC has warned that widening hostilities are putting civilian lives in grave danger. No confirmed reports in the last 24 hours indicate a major new humanitarian access breakthrough, suggesting aid operations remain constrained by insecurity and damaged infrastructure. ([ir.icrc.org](https://ir.icrc.org/en/2026/03/icrc-president-widening-hostilities-across-middle-east-putting-civilian-lives-in-grave-danger/?utm_source=openai))

Outlook: For March 14, the key indicators to watch are whether coalition forces shift toward another intensified strike cycle, whether Iran attempts a fresh large-scale missile or maritime action, and whether any escorted commercial transit through Hormuz resumes. If oil remains pinned near or above $100 and shipping stays choked, economic pressure may begin to rival battlefield developments as the conflict’s main escalatory driver. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/99a2316e9089b2ffa9eadd104e787591?utm_source=openai))

briefingArchive.title: Day 12 Intelligence Briefing — March 12, 2026
briefingArchive.content:
The past 24 hours brought no clear sign of de-escalation in the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran. Instead, the most significant development was a continued pattern of Israeli strikes on missile and command infrastructure in and around Tehran, paired with repeated Iranian missile launches toward Israel and growing evidence that the war’s wider economic and maritime effects are deepening faster than the military balance is clarifying. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

## Military Operations Summary

In the last 24 hours, the clearest confirmed operational reporting came from the Israeli military rather than from CENTCOM. The IDF said it conducted another overnight wave of strikes in Tehran, including against an IRGC underground research-and-development complex within the Imam Hussein military university, infrastructure at the main Quds Force headquarters, and additional weapons and defense-production sites. The IDF also said it carried out multiple time-sensitive attacks on ballistic missile launchers and launch cells in western Iran, claiming some were struck minutes before launch and that more than 70 ready-to-fire launchers had been dismantled or thwarted since the operation began. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

Iran, meanwhile, continued missile launches toward Israeli territory through the day, prompting repeated Home Front Command warnings. The IDF reported several launch detections and said defensive systems were activated multiple times; it also acknowledged that at least two interception attempts on rockets fired from Lebanon toward central Israel failed the previous day, causing impacts and light civilian injuries. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

At sea, pressure on Gulf shipping intensified. USNI, citing the Joint Maritime Information Center and Reuters-linked reporting, said three cargo ships were damaged in suspected Iranian attacks in the Strait of Hormuz on March 11 and that 17 ships had been hit there since February 28. Separately, the UN said ship traffic through Hormuz had dropped 97% since February 28, underscoring that the maritime campaign is now one of the war’s most consequential operational theaters. ([news.usni.org](https://news.usni.org/2026/03/11/3-cargo-ships-damaged-in-suspected-iranian-attacks-in-the-strait-of-hormuz?utm_source=openai))

## What Each Side Is Reporting

Public reporting remains highly asymmetric. U.S. official channels have been comparatively sparse in the last 24 hours. CENTCOM’s most accessible recent public baseline remains its March 6 fact sheet, which said Operation Epic Fury began at 1:15 a.m. on February 28, had struck more than 3,000 targets in its first seven days, and had damaged or destroyed 43 Iranian ships. However, no comparable detailed CENTCOM day-12 operational summary was readily available in the last 24 hours. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/Portals/6/Documents/Publications/260306EpicFuryFactSheet7Days.pdf))

Israel is providing the most granular battlefield narrative. The IDF’s updates portray a campaign focused on suppressing Iranian ballistic missile fire, degrading IRGC command nodes, and expanding strikes into Hezbollah-linked financial and command infrastructure in Lebanon. This framing emphasizes pre-emption, rapid kill chains, and precision targeting. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

Iranian state-aligned media present a sharply different picture. Press TV coverage in the last 24 hours stressed U.S. and allied interceptor depletion, citing claims that more than 1,000 Patriot PAC-3 interceptors have been fired and portraying Iran’s missile-drone campaign as strategically sustainable. Other recent Iranian official messaging has flatly rejected the U.S. justification that Iran was preparing imminent attacks, calling that rationale “a sheer and utter lie.” ([presstv.ir](https://www.presstv.ir/Detail/2026/03/11/765264/US-burns-through-two-years-worth-of-Patriot-missile-production-in-war-on-Iran-Report?utm_source=openai))

The contradictions are significant. Israel highlights launcher destruction and command-node attrition; Iranian media emphasize continuing launch capacity and mounting U.S. costs. Neither side’s combat claims can be fully verified independently at tactical level. The best-supported analytic judgment is that coalition airpower retains freedom to strike deep targets, but Iran still preserves enough surviving missile capability to sustain periodic retaliatory fire and to impose serious costs in the maritime domain. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

## Market & Economic Impact

Energy markets remain the most sensitive real-time barometer of the conflict. Reuters-reported pricing from March 10 showed Brent crude falling sharply to about $86.50 a barrel after having surged to the highest levels since 2022 the day before; AP also reported oil had earlier whipped from nearly $120 back below $90 as traders oscillated between fears of prolonged disruption and hopes for de-escalation. By March 10, UN officials said Brent had risen above $90 per barrel following recent disruptions, showing that even after the retreat from the peak, the war premium remains large. ([investing.com](https://www.investing.com/news/commodities-news/oil-prices-sink-13-as-trump-predicts-middle-east-deescalation-4552840?utm_source=openai))

Equity markets have stabilized only partially. AP said the S&P 500 slipped 0.1% on Wednesday, March 11, after two sessions of smaller moves following the earlier war-driven volatility. Earlier in the week, the index had fallen 0.2% Tuesday after a dramatic Monday rebound in which the S&P 500 rose 0.8% to 6,795.99. The pattern suggests investors are no longer pricing immediate systemic collapse, but they are still reacting to each signal on war duration and energy flows. ([apnews.com](https://apnews.com/article/ca4cf546735077387e97304039299532?utm_source=openai))

Safe-haven behavior continues as well. Gold prices were reported above $5,100 an ounce on March 6 in market coverage, though no authoritative fresh benchmark was readily confirmed in the last 24 hours. On balance, the global macro picture remains defined by three linked risks: sustained oil volatility, higher inflation expectations, and transport disruptions centered on Hormuz. ([theguardian.com](https://www.theguardian.com/business/live/2026/mar/06/oil-biggest-weekly-gain-four-years-strait-of-hormuz-traffic-halt-stock-markets-dollar-imf-news-updates?filterKeyEvents=false&page=with%3Ablock-69aa90328f080482c628bef8&utm_source=openai))

## Consumer & Supply Chain Effects

The consumer-facing effects are becoming more visible, even if some of the most dramatic hoarding narratives in Asia remain thinly confirmed in open sources over the last 24 hours. No confirmed major new reports in the last 24 hours were found of mass retail fuel hoarding in Japan, Singapore, or other Asia-Pacific economies from authoritative primary reporting. That said, broader indicators point to clear supply-chain stress. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/sg/en/content/highlight/2026-03-10.html?utm_source=openai))

The most important data point is maritime. UN officials, citing UNCTAD, said ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has fallen 97% since February 28. The strait carries roughly a quarter of global seaborne oil and about one-third of global seaborne fertilizer trade, so even partial disruption has outsized downstream effects on freight, insurance, refinery scheduling, and consumer fuel pricing. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/sg/en/content/highlight/2026-03-10.html?utm_source=openai))

Shipping risk has also become physical rather than merely financial. USNI reported three cargo ships damaged in suspected Iranian attacks on March 11, with one vessel continuing underway, another anchored with hull damage, and crew members reportedly trapped in an engine room on a third. This follows a cumulative count of 17 ships hit in Hormuz since the war began. Such incidents are likely to reinforce rerouting, delay chartering decisions, and drive up war-risk premiums. ([news.usni.org](https://news.usni.org/2026/03/11/3-cargo-ships-damaged-in-suspected-iranian-attacks-in-the-strait-of-hormuz?utm_source=openai))

For consumers, the clearest immediate effect remains higher fuel prices rather than outright shortage. U.S. local reporting has already shown gasoline price spikes, and if Hormuz disruption persists for another several days, Asia-Pacific importers with less pricing insulation will likely see sharper retail impacts and precautionary buying. That is an analytic forecast, not a confirmed current condition. ([axios.com](https://www.axios.com/local/detroit/2026/03/05/metro-detroit-gas-prices-jump-iran-oil-spike?utm_source=openai))

## Diplomatic Developments

Diplomatically, the day’s picture is one of activity without breakthrough. The UN Security Council met on March 11 to discuss the escalating crisis, while Secretary-General António Guterres and senior UN officials continued urging restraint and renewed diplomacy. The UN system is clearly trying to move the conversation from battlefield momentum toward a ceasefire framework, but there is no confirmed ceasefire agreement or formal negotiation track producing visible results in the last 24 hours. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/en/middle-east-live-11-march-more-strikes-calls-grow-peace?utm_source=openai))

Iranian messaging remains publicly defiant. Recent Press TV reporting reiterated Tehran’s position that it has exchanged no messages with Washington since the war began and that it is focused on “legitimate self-defense.” Earlier Iranian reporting also argued that prewar Geneva diplomacy had been undercut by the U.S.-Israeli attack, reinforcing Tehran’s narrative that negotiations were exploited rather than pursued in good faith. ([presstv.ir](https://www.presstv.ir/Detail/2026/03/05/764983/Iran-deputy-FM-says-no-messages-exchanged-with-US-since-war?utm_source=openai))

On the multilateral side, the UN highlighted the global trade risks of a prolonged Hormuz crisis, which may increase pressure on Gulf states, China, and major energy importers to push quietly for de-escalation. However, no authoritative confirmation emerged in the last 24 hours of successful back-channel talks involving Oman, Qatar, or other intermediaries. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/sg/en/content/highlight/2026-03-10.html?utm_source=openai))

Analytically, diplomacy appears constrained by both sides’ battlefield narratives. Israel and the United States still appear to believe additional military pressure can further reduce Iranian retaliatory capacity, while Tehran appears intent on proving it can outlast the initial shock and impose regional costs. That dynamic is not favorable to an immediate ceasefire. ([idf.il](https://www.idf.il/en/mini-sites/operation-roaring-lion/real-time-updates-day-by-day/march-10-2026-real-time-updates-operation-roaring-lion/))

## Humanitarian Situation

The humanitarian picture continues to worsen, though verified same-day figures remain difficult to update cleanly. UN reporting on March 11 described rising civilian tolls, mounting humanitarian needs, strained services, and regional disruption affecting mobility, trade, and aid operations. UNHCR also published a regional situation update dated March 11, indicating the conflict’s effects now extend across Iran and multiple neighboring states. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/en/middle-east-live-11-march-more-strikes-calls-grow-peace?utm_source=openai))

The most widely cited casualty figures still appear to come from Iranian official channels and secondary reporting rather than independently audited international bodies. South China Morning Post, citing Iran’s health ministry and other official sources, reported that more than 1,200 people had been killed in Iran by March 10 and that over 10,000 civilians had been injured; another widely cited figure put the toll at 1,230. Those figures should be treated as plausible but not independently verified. ([scmp.com](https://www.scmp.com/news/world/middle-east/article/3346059/casualty-figures-across-middle-east-iran-war-enters-second-week?utm_source=openai))

Civilian infrastructure damage is also accumulating. Al Jazeera, citing AP imagery and reporting, highlighted hospital damage in Tehran. Amnesty International said Israeli strikes on fuel storage facilities in Tehran and Alborz had caused civilian deaths and injuries, and warned all parties against unlawful attacks on energy infrastructure. ([aljazeera.com](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/3/11/iran-reports-hospitals-civilians-affected-during-war-with-us-israel?utm_source=openai))

Displacement is rising regionally, but precise fresh totals remain incomplete in open sources. UNHCR flash reporting over recent days has indicated cross-border movement into Iraq and growing contingency planning around possible larger outflows from Iran. The humanitarian trajectory is therefore negative even in the absence of a fully consolidated day-12 count. ([unhcr.org](https://www.unhcr.org/sites/default/files/2026-03/UNHCR-Middle-East-situation-emergency-flash-update-02.pdf?utm_source=openai))

Outlook: For March 13, the key indicators to watch are whether Israeli strikes expand further into regime-core targets in Tehran, whether Iran sustains missile salvos at current tempo, and whether additional ship attacks force a de facto closure of Hormuz traffic. The market signal to monitor most closely is whether Brent decisively moves back above $90-$100 a barrel, which would suggest traders increasingly expect maritime disruption to outlast the current phase of air operations.

briefingArchive.title: Day 11 Intelligence Briefing — March 11, 2026
briefingArchive.content:
The most significant development over the past 24 hours appears to be a dual escalation at sea and in the air: fresh attacks on commercial shipping in and near the Strait of Hormuz coincided with continued US-Israeli strikes inside Iran and renewed Iranian missile and drone salvos across Israel and Gulf targets. Markets have reacted with continued energy volatility rather than outright panic, as traders weigh the growing physical disruption to Gulf shipping against emerging signs of diplomatic probing around a possible ceasefire. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2026/03/11/strait-hormuz-cargo-ships-iran/?utm_source=openai))

## Military Operations Summary

In the last 24 hours, confirmed reporting points to continued high-tempo US/coalition air operations against Iranian military and dual-use targets, alongside Iranian retaliation across multiple theaters. CENTCOM has not, in the search results available today, published a same-day detailed battle damage update for March 11, but its official fact sheet and prior statements establish that Operation Epic Fury has focused on Iranian command-and-control nodes, air defenses, missile and drone launch sites, military airfields, and naval assets. CENTCOM also previously confirmed the use of air-, land-, and sea-launched precision munitions and one-way attack drones under Task Force Scorpion Strike. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4418396/us-forces-launch-operation-epic-fury/?utm_source=openai))

Independent military tracking suggests March 11 strikes hit a Bank Sepah building, the 7th Artesh Air Force Fighter Base, and at least one radar site near Bandar Abbas, indicating the campaign continues to target Iran’s military sustainment, air-defense picture, and financial-military linkages rather than only fixed missile infrastructure. Critical Threats also assessed that Iranian media imagery showed a likely Iranian unmanned surface vessel impact on the Thai-flagged Mayuree Naree in the Strait of Hormuz, while UKMTO-linked reporting cited at least two other probable attacks on commercial shipping today. ([criticalthreats.org](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/iran-update-morning-special-report-march-11-2026?utm_source=openai))

On the naval side, Anadolu reported President Trump saying US forces destroyed 10 Iranian mine-laying vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, a claim that, if accurate, suggests the maritime fight has shifted from deterrence patrols to active mine-counterforce operations. Iranian retaliation also continued against Israel and Gulf-state or US-linked targets; Israeli and outside assessments indicate Iran is still launching repeated barrages, though with fewer missiles per salvo than in the war’s opening phase. ([aa.com.tr](https://www.aa.com.tr/en/live/morning-briefing-march-11-2026/3859069?utm_source=openai))

## What Each Side Is Reporting

US and coalition messaging remains centered on precision, force protection, and maritime security. CENTCOM’s official launch statement described the campaign as aimed at dismantling Iran’s security apparatus and prioritizing targets posing an imminent threat, including IRGC command facilities, air defenses, missile and drone sites, and military airfields. Earlier CENTCOM updates also acknowledged US casualties during the operation, including a service member wounded in Saudi Arabia and seven killed in action by March 8, undercutting any suggestion from official Washington that the campaign has been cost-free. ([centcom.mil](https://www.centcom.mil/MEDIA/PRESS-RELEASES/Press-Release-View/Article/4418396/us-forces-launch-operation-epic-fury/?utm_source=openai))

The White House framing is broader and more maximalist, characterizing Operation Epic Fury as an effort to eliminate Iran’s nuclear threat, destroy ballistic missile capacity, degrade proxy networks, and cripple naval forces. That language goes beyond a narrow retaliatory or defensive mission and indicates a strategic-degradation objective. ([whitehouse.gov](https://www.whitehouse.gov/articles/2026/03/peace-through-strength-president-trump-launches-operation-epic-fury-to-crush-iranian-regime-end-nuclear-threat/?utm_source=openai))

Iranian state and aligned media are presenting a sharply different picture. Press TV today emphasized that the US has already consumed the equivalent of two years of Patriot missile production and claimed damage to a THAAD radar in Jordan, while EFE reported Iran describing its latest attacks as the “most devastating and hardest” wave yet. Iranian outlets also insist Tehran is striking only military targets and accuse the United States of using civilian populations in neighboring states as “human shields.” These claims are difficult to verify independently and should be treated as information operations unless corroborated. ([presstv.ir](https://www.presstv.ir/Detail/2026/03/11/765264/US-burns-through-two-years-worth-of-Patriot-missile-production-in-war-on-Iran-Report?utm_source=openai))

Israeli and outside reporting add another layer. Israeli-linked assessments cited by Critical Threats indicate Iran’s daily missile volume is declining, perhaps reflecting interceptor pressure, launcher attrition, or stockpile management. The contradiction is therefore clear: Washington stresses precision and progress, Tehran stresses resilience and heavy US losses, and Israel stresses shrinking Iranian capacity. All three narratives contain obvious propaganda incentives. ([criticalthreats.org](https://www.criticalthreats.org/analysis/iran-update-morning-special-report-march-11-2026?utm_source=openai))

## Market & Economic Impact

Energy markets remain the clearest transmission channel from the conflict into the global economy. As of March 11, Brent crude was reported around $89.99 a barrel, up 2.6% in one market snapshot, while other reporting showed crude prices rebounding as additional vessels were attacked near Hormuz. Earlier this week, Brent had traded near or above the low-$90s, and Reuters reported on March 10 that the US Energy Information Administration now expects Brent to remain above $95 for the next two months because of war-related disruption before easing later in 2026. ([local10.com](https://www.local10.com/business/2026/03/11/world-shares-are-mixed-as-investors-watch-for-release-of-oil-reserves/?utm_source=openai))

The market tone today is volatile but not disorderly. Investors appear to be pricing a prolonged logistics shock rather than a complete and sustained energy cutoff. UNCTAD warned yesterday that Hormuz disruptions threaten not only oil and LNG but also fertilizers and vulnerable import-dependent economies, reinforcing the idea that this is now a broad commodity and trade shock rather than a pure oil story. ([unctad.org](https://unctad.org/news/hormuz-shipping-disruptions-raise-risks-energy-fertilizers-and-vulnerable-economies?utm_source=openai))

Equities have shown mixed rather than universally risk-off behavior. A market roundup published early March 11 said world shares were mixed as investors awaited a major emergency release of oil reserves, suggesting that expectations of government intervention have moderated some of the worst-case pricing. Defense names, by contrast, have rallied on expectations of sustained munitions demand and a longer conflict horizon. ([local10.com](https://www.local10.com/business/2026/03/11/world-shares-are-mixed-as-investors-watch-for-release-of-oil-reserves/?utm_source=openai))

A major stabilizing factor is the emergency reserve response. Reporting today indicated the IEA’s 32 member states agreed to release 400 million barrels from strategic reserves, with Japan alone preparing to release around 80 million barrels beginning March 18. That should cap upside in spot prices somewhat, but it does not solve the physical risk to tankers, insurers, and port operations if attacks in and around Hormuz continue. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_Strait_of_Hormuz_crisis?utm_source=openai))

## Consumer & Supply Chain Effects

Consumer effects are increasingly visible across Asia-Pacific, especially in fuel-import-dependent economies. ABC Australia reported on March 10 that petrol prices have surged across Asia, traders are scrambling for alternative supply, and long lines have formed at fuel stations in Sri Lanka amid fears of shortages. That is one of the clearest confirmed indicators that the conflict is already moving from macro-market stress into retail behavior and precautionary buying. ([abc.net.au](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-03-10/petrol-prices-surge-asia-middle-east-war-iran-us-israel/106432518?utm_source=openai))

On the shipping side, the Strait of Hormuz is functioning far below normal throughput. Windward assessed today that only seven vessels had crossed the strait since March 6 and described a maritime environment of suppressed traffic, widening attack patterns, and growing mine-deployment risk. Reuters-sourced reporting from last week already showed majors and traders suspending shipments via Hormuz, while Maersk announced emergency freight increases and operational suspensions affecting cargo to and from Gulf destinations. ([windward.ai](https://windward.ai/blog/march-11-maritime-intelligence-daily/?utm_source=openai))

Physical risk to merchant shipping rose further on March 11. The Washington Post reported several cargo ships were struck by projectiles, and Critical Threats assessed the Mayuree Naree was likely hit near the rudder and propeller area in a disabling strike profile. Such attacks matter disproportionately because even a small number of successful incidents can sharply raise war-risk premiums, reduce available hulls, and induce self-deterring behavior by shipowners. ([washingtonpost.com](https://www.washingtonpost.com/world/2026/03/11/strait-hormuz-cargo-ships-iran/?utm_source=openai))

The broader supply-chain effect is that rerouting options are limited. While some Gulf producers can push more barrels via Red Sea or pipeline alternatives, not all volumes are substitutable. UNCTAD warned that vulnerable economies face knock-on effects in food, fertilizer, and industrial inputs, meaning the conflict’s logistics shock is broadening beyond crude and refined fuels. ([unctad.org](https://unctad.org/news/hormuz-shipping-disruptions-raise-risks-energy-fertilizers-and-vulnerable-economies?utm_source=openai))

## Diplomatic Developments

Diplomatically, the picture is fluid but still short of formal ceasefire negotiations. The most notable development is Iran’s own acknowledgment, via Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi, that China, Russia, and France have contacted Tehran regarding a possible ceasefire or political off-ramp. Multiple outlets carried that claim on March 10, and while it remains second-hand, it is the clearest sign in the last 24 hours of active diplomatic probing by major powers. ([plataformamedia.com](https://www.plataformamedia.com/en/2026/03/10/china-russia-france-contact-iran-ceasefire-middle-east-war/?utm_source=openai))

There is, however, no sign Tehran has accepted a ceasefire framework. Earlier Reuters-linked reporting quoted Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi saying Iran was not seeking a ceasefire with the US or Israel and saw no reason to negotiate with Washington under current conditions. That stance suggests Tehran wants to preserve coercive leverage while absorbing strikes, not signal weakness prematurely. ([tbsnews.net](https://www.tbsnews.net/world/tehran-says-no-ceasefire-refuses-negotiations-us-1378746?utm_source=openai))

At the UN, emergency diplomacy continues. The Security Council met in emergency session after the initial strikes, and international messaging has focused on de-escalation, civilian protection, and maritime security. China’s stated line, as cited in today’s Alma briefing, is opposition to war expansion and condemnation of harm to civilians and non-military targets. France, meanwhile, has publicly distanced itself from the US-Israeli strikes, with Anadolu reporting Paris saying it “cannot approve” them. ([un.org](https://www.un.org/en/middle-east-live-un-security-council-meeting-emergency-session-over-iran?utm_source=openai))

Regionally, Gulf states remain caught between hosting US assets and trying to prevent further spillover. Earlier emergency GCC messaging demanded Iran halt attacks and asserted self-defense rights, while current regional risk reporting shows Gulf civil aviation, port, and military postures still adjusting daily. Back-channel contacts via Oman remain plausible, but there are no confirmed reports in the last 24 hours of a structured negotiation round. ([ksat.com](https://www.ksat.com/news/world/2026/03/02/world-hurries-to-respond-to-rapidly-changing-war-around-iran/?utm_source=openai))

## Humanitarian Situation

The humanitarian picture remains severe, but verified same-day numbers are limited and highly politicized. No neutral body in the search results published a comprehensive March 11 casualty update for the last 24 hours alone. Iranian officials and state-linked reporting continue to cite very high civilian losses; Xinhua summarized Iran’s UN envoy as saying over 1,300 Iranian civilians have been killed and nearly 10,000 civilian sites destroyed in US-Israeli strikes. Those numbers should be treated as claims unless independently verified. ([english.news.cn](https://english.news.cn/20260311/0e8440a9ae244996b5a4ef084f957902/c.html?utm_source=openai))

Broader reporting does support a heavy civilian toll. The National, drawing on Reuters reporting earlier in the war, documented rising strikes near homes, businesses, and heritage sites. Earlier Iranian Red Crescent-linked figures referenced hundreds of deaths and thousands of damaged civilian units, while state media have claimed the destruction of roughly 13,000 civilian units. The exact totals remain contested, but the pattern is clear: the war is no longer confined to isolated military compounds. ([thenationalnews.com](https://www.thenationalnews.com/news/mena/2026/03/03/civilian-cost-of-us-israeli-war-mounts-in-iran//?utm_source=openai))

The humanitarian burden is not limited to Iran. Iranian strikes have caused civilian casualties and infrastructure disruption in Gulf states, including Kuwait, Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, Oman, and the UAE, though at much smaller scale than the reported impact inside Iran. Solace Global reported at least six killed and dozens injured in Kuwait as of early March 11, while other earlier reports confirmed civilian deaths from debris and fires elsewhere in the Gulf. ([solaceglobal.com](https://www.solaceglobal.com/news/2026/03/11/gulf-sitrep-1103/?utm_source=openai))

No confirmed UNHCR or ICRC displacement bulletin specific to the last 24 hours was surfaced in the search results. The absence of updated agency figures should not be read as stabilization; rather, it reflects the lag between fast-moving strike activity and formal humanitarian accounting. ([nbcconnecticut.com](https://www.nbcconnecticut.com/news/national-international/iran-us-israel-middle-east-conflict-march-11-2026/3712680/?utm_source=openai))

Outlook

For March 12, the key indicators to watch are whether the US expands maritime strike activity against Iranian mine-laying and small-boat assets, whether Iran sustains attacks on commercial shipping, and whether the reported diplomatic outreach from China, Russia, and France coalesces into an identifiable ceasefire track. Markets will be watching the same three variables: tanker risk in Hormuz, the credibility of the IEA reserve release, and whether Iran’s apparently reduced missile-salvo size reflects real attrition or merely a temporary operational pause.
